step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

Asking a patient to rate their confidence in quitting on a scale of 1 to 10, and then asking what would make it one point higher, is a simple way to identify what support or information they feel they are missing.

Therapist: Since you're open to the idea of talking to your coach and wanting to stay focused without smoking, I think it might be helpful to practice a simple phrase to say when someone offers you a cigarette, especially in social situations. Would you be willing to try saying "no, thanks" and reminding yourself why you quit when someone invites you to smoke?
Patient: It sounds kinda simple, but I don't know if I can just say no without feeling weird. What if they ask me why, and I don't have a good reason?

Therapist: That's a valid concern about not having a good reason to say no, and it can be tough to come up with an answer on the spot. Can you think of any reasons related to your team or goals that you could use when someone asks why you're not smoking?

Patient: I can tell them it's because I want to improve my game for the team. That way, I'm not lying or anything, and it's related to why I want to quit in the first place. Maybe it'll even sound cool to them or something.
Therapist: Now that you have a reason that feels authentic to you and related to your goals, can you imagine how you might respond if someone asks you to smoke in a social situation?

Patient: I'd say something like, "Nah, I'm trying to stay sharp for the team. I want to make sure I'm doing everything I can to help us win." It sounds pretty good in my head, but what if they don't believe me or think I'm being fake?

turns.user_message: If you think the coach is behind me trying to quit, that would help a lot. If he's on my side, it'd show that it's not just me being weird, that it's something we're all working towards together. That would make me feel more confident, I think.
step2_rag_chunks: It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.

step7_response: You've come up with a plan to talk to your coach about your goals. How do you think it would feel to have your coach's support and encouragement in place before you need to say no to a cigarette in a social situation?

turns.user_message: It'd be a huge weight off my shoulders, I think. Knowing the coach has my back would give me so much more confidence to say no to cigarettes, and it would feel like I've got a lot less to lose.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
